1:
Ps 77: Asaph's Journey from Lament to Exodus Memory. The psalm is attributed to Asaph and directed 'to Jeduthun' — Jeduthun (also called Ethan) was one of the three chief Levitical musician guilds alongside Asaph and Heman (1 Chronicles 16:41-42; Psalms 25:1-6). The Jeduthun guild is associated with Pss 39, 62, 77. The psalm has a precise two-part structure: personal lament (vv1-9) and historical recollection of the Exodus (vv11-20), joined by the theological pivot of v10. This structure — crisis drives the worshiper back to foundational salvation history — is the Psalter's characteristic response to present suffering.

'I cried out to God with my voice' — qoli el-Elohim (my voice to God) — the cry is physical and loud. The redundancy ('cried with my voice') emphasizes the urgency and audibility of the lament: not merely inward anxiety but voiced protest directed toward God.
2: 'In the day of my trouble I seek the Lord; in the night my hand is stretched out without wearying; my soul refuses to be comforted' — 'my hand stretched out without wearying' (yadi laylah nigerah velo tapug) — the hand extended in prayer posture through the night without stopping (the gesture of Exodus 17:11-12, Moses' hands raised in prayer during the battle). 'My soul refuses to be comforted' (meat hinnachem naphshi) — the most desperate description of grief in the Psalter: the usual mechanisms of comfort (prayer, community, ritual) have failed. The soul has closed itself off from consolation.
3: 'When I remember God, I moan; when I meditate, my spirit faints' — the reversal of normal theology: remembering God produces not comfort but groaning. In the lament psalms, the memory of God is supposed to generate confidence (cf. Psalms 42:4-8); here it does the opposite. The crisis is so acute that even the thought of God increases the pain. This is the experience of divine abandonment — where the God who should be the source of rescue seems instead to be the source of the distress.
4: 'You hold my eyelids open; I am so troubled that I cannot speak' — 'you hold my eyelids open' (achazta shemurot einai) — YHWH himself prevents the psalmist from sleeping. The insomnia is divine: God keeps the eyes open. The sleeplessness that in Psalms 63:6 produces joyful nighttime meditation here produces nothing but speechless anguish. 'Cannot speak' (niphamu velo adabber) — the pre-lament silence is worse than the lament: the tongue cannot even find the words of distress.
5: 'I consider the days of old, the years long ago' — the first movement toward salvation history: the psalmist begins to look backward. 'Days of old' (yemei qedem) and 'years of long ago' (shenot olamim) — the deep past of YHWH's saving acts. This retrospection is the structural bridge from individual lament (vv1-4) toward the Exodus recollection (vv11-20). The theological strategy: when the present is unreadable, seek the God of the past.
6: 'I said, Let me remember my song in the night; let me meditate in my heart. Then my spirit made a diligent search' — the nighttime song as the object of meditation. 'My song in the night' (shirti balaylah) — probably a lament or praise song known from the sanctuary that the psalmist rehearses in the dark. 'My spirit made a diligent search' (vayechafer ruchi, Piel of chapar = to dig, search carefully) — an intensive investigation of the spirit: probing one's own interior for some vestige of the salvation-experience.
7: 'Will the Lord spurn forever, and never again be favorable?' — the first of seven questions (vv7-9) that mark the nadir of the psalm. These questions are not rhetorical but genuine: the psalmist does not know the answers. They probe the possibility that YHWH's covenant character has fundamentally changed. In ANE thought, a patron deity could permanently abandon a devotee — divine faithfulness was not unconditional. The psalmist brings this possibility before YHWH as the most terrifying hypothesis.
8: 'Has his steadfast love forever ceased? Are his promises at an end for all time?' — 'his steadfast love forever ceased' (gamer chesed leolam) — gavar (to be complete, come to an end) applied to chesed: has the covenant-love run out, been used up? The permanence of divine chesed is the foundational promise (Ps 136: 'his steadfast love endures forever'); the question interrogates that permanence. 'His promises at an end' (gamar omer ledor vador) — the divine speech-act that created the covenant: has the word stopped?
9: 'Has God forgotten to be gracious? Has he in anger shut up his compassion?' — 'Has God forgotten to be gracious?' (hashach chonnot El) — divine forgetfulness is the theological impossibility the psalmist dares to voice. YHWH's name is 'gracious' (channun) by self-definition (Exodus 34:6); has he forgotten his own character? 'Shut up his compassion' (qaphatz beaph rachamav) — the womb-mercy (rachamim) sealed off. Seven questions, seven painful lances into the covenant's reliability. The sevenfold structure mirrors the seven-day creation: YHWH created the world in seven acts of blessing; has he now sealed it in seven acts of withdrawal?
10: 'Then I said, It is my grief that the right hand of the Most High has changed' — the theological pivot of the entire psalm. Chaloti hi — this verse is difficult and contested; the MKT reads it as the psalmist's self-diagnosis: 'This is my grief/wound — the changing of the right hand of the Most High.' The key insight: the problem may be the psalmist's perception, not a change in YHWH. YHWH's right hand — the hand of saving action — has not actually changed; the psalmist's perspective has been distorted by suffering. This opens the door to the second movement of the psalm: recall what the right hand has actually done.
11: 'I will remember the deeds of the LORD; yes, I will remember your wonders of old' — the decisive turn: ezkor maale-Yah (I will remember the deeds of Yah). The first person singular future (ezkor, I will remember) is an act of will, a choice made against the grain of the painful experience. 'Wonders of old' (miphleotechah miqqedem) — the niphlaot (wondrous acts) are specifically the Exodus events: the sea-splitting, the pillar of fire, the plagues. The psalmist deliberately anchors personal crisis in the community's foundational salvation narrative.
12: 'I will ponder all your work, and meditate on your mighty deeds' — vehaggiti (I will meditate, voiced self-talk) and asicha (I will contemplate) — both verbs of deliberate sustained attention. The psalmist commits to the practice of meditation (hagah, the same word as Psalms 1:2) on the divine acts. The practice of meditative rehearsal of salvation history is the cognitive-liturgical practice that transforms lament into praise.
13: 'Your way, O God, is holy. What god is great like our God?' — baqodesh darkechah El (in holiness is your way, O God) — YHWH's way/path (derek) is characterized by holiness: moral purity, separation from all that corrupts. The rhetorical question — 'what god is great like our God?' (mi El gadol ke-Elohim) — is a direct polemic against ANE polytheism: YHWH is incomparable. Exodus 15:11 uses the same rhetorical question immediately after the sea-crossing: 'Who is like you, O LORD, among the gods?'
14: 'You are the God who works wonders; you have made known your might among the peoples' — El oseh phele (God who does wonders) — the same phele/niphla vocabulary as vv11-12: the wondrous acts are now identified as YHWH's specific divine quality. 'Made known your might among the peoples' (hoda-ta baamim uzechah) — the Exodus events were witnessed by Egypt and the surrounding peoples: Exodus 9:16 ('I have raised you up to show my power, so that my name may be proclaimed in all the earth'), Joshua 2:9-11 (Rahab's testimony of the nations hearing about YHWH's deeds).
15: 'You with your arm redeemed your people, the children of Jacob and Joseph' — 'the arm of YHWH' (zero'a) is the Exodus vocabulary: Exodus 6:6 ('I will redeem you with an outstretched arm'); Deuteronomy 4:34; Psalms 26:8. The divine arm is the instrument of salvific power. 'Children of Jacob and Joseph' — the double patriarchal reference is unusual: normally 'Jacob and Israel.' Joseph represents the northern tribes (Ephraim and Manasseh) — the inclusion of Joseph alongside Jacob suggests the psalm addresses all twelve tribes, reunited in the memory of the common Exodus ancestor.
16: 'When the waters saw you, O God, when the waters saw you, they were afraid; indeed, the deep trembled' — the cosmic sea-splitting recollection begins. The waters as sentient respondents to divine presence: they 'saw' (rauchah) and 'were afraid' (yachailu). This is the mythological-poetic register of the Exodus: the sea does not merely part because of wind (Exodus 14:21) but actively responds to divine presence with fear. The 'deep' (tehomot) — the primordial depth of creation (Genesis 1:2) — trembled. Creation itself responds to the creator's act of redemption.
17: 'The clouds poured out water; the skies gave forth thunder; your arrows flashed on every side' — the Exodus theophany as cosmic storm: thunder (qol natenu shaqim, the skies gave their voice), arrows (chitzechah = lightning). This is the same storm-language of Ps 29 applied to the Exodus event: YHWH as the divine storm-lord demonstrating his power at the sea. Exodus 14:24 records that YHWH looked from the pillar of fire and cloud and threw the Egyptian army into panic — the storm-theophany is part of the Exodus narrative.
18: 'The crash of your thunder was in the whirlwind; your lightnings lit up the world; the earth trembled and shook' — the full Sinai-type theophany applied to the sea-event. The 'whirlwind' (galgal, the wheel or whirling) — the divine chariot-storm (cf. Ezek 1). The earth trembling and shaking (ratash vetirash haaretz) is the cosmic response to divine presence. The Psalter consistently reads the Exodus as a creation-event: YHWH's saving of Israel is as cosmically significant as the original creation, requiring the same response from the created order.
19: 'Your way was through the sea, your path through the great waters; yet your footprints were unseen' — the most theologically suggestive verse of the psalm. YHWH's path was through the sea, yet 'your footprints were unseen' (ve'ikevotechah lo noda'u). The divine way is real but invisible: YHWH acts in history in ways that leave no directly traceable footprints. The sea closed again after Israel passed through — the path was obliterated. This is a theology of divine hiddenness: YHWH is sovereignly active but not empirically traceable. The faith of v11 ('I will remember') is required precisely because the footprints are unseen.
20: 'You led your people like a flock by the hand of Moses and Aaron' — the psalm closes with the shepherd-image of divine leadership. YHWH as shepherd, Moses and Aaron as the human instruments. The Exodus rescue is not directly executed by Moses or Aaron but by YHWH 'through their hand' (beyad Moshe veAharon) — divine agency through human mediation. The flock image echoes Psalms 78:52; 80:1 and connects to the Good Shepherd language of John 10 and the shepherd-king theology of Ezek 34. The lament that opened with 'my soul refuses to be comforted' (v2) closes with the image of the divine shepherd comforting and leading the flock: the Exodus memory provides what personal experience could not.
